case_name: Potable reuse polishing for surface water impacted by Metolachlor ethane sulfonic acid, dichlorvos, and tebuconazole under moderate budget and limited-operator constraints
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of treatment process chains for surface water intended for potable reuse that can control Metolachlor Degradates, dichlorvos, and tebuconazole while balancing cost, operator burden, residuals, and reliability.

input_data.scenario_background: A regional water utility is upgrading a surface-water-based reuse train to provide an advanced polishing barrier for indirect potable reuse after watershed runoff and upstream return flows introduced pesticide-related contaminants. Metolachlor ethane sulfonic acid is the primary recurring compound, while dichlorvos and tebuconazole appear seasonally at lower concentrations. The utility has competent conventional-treatment staff but limited experience with highly complex advanced treatment systems, so the preferred solution must balance multi-contaminant removal, moderate capital and operating budgets, and manageable residuals. The trade-off is that adsorption-based options are simpler but may be less robust for highly polar degradates, while membrane routes are technically strong but create concentrate and operational burdens.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should provide a robust potable-reuse barrier with very low pesticide and pesticide-degradate concentrations and stable compliance under variable seasonal source conditions.
secondary_goals: maintain manageable operations for staff with limited advanced-treatment experience
avoid highly difficult concentrate disposal where possible
provide resilience to seasonal contaminant spikes and changing organic matter
limit formation of oxidation byproducts requiring complex downstream control

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: prefer spent media or backwash residuals over high-volume membrane concentrate, and avoid difficult hazardous residual handling where feasible
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: treatment must tolerate seasonal changes in turbidity, DOC, and pesticide mixture while maintaining a dependable multi-barrier potable reuse performance

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: reduce turbidity and a portion of organic matter to stabilize downstream advanced treatment
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: polish suspended solids and protect ozone and BAC units
core_unit_functions.ozonation: oxidize dichlorvos and tebuconazole and transform biodegradable organics
core_unit_functions.biological_activated_carbon: adsorb residual hydrophobic organics and biologically remove ozone-transformation products and some trace organics
core_unit_functions.UV_disinfection: provide final pathogen barrier for potable reuse

applicability_rationale: this train balances trace-organic control with moderate cost and manageable operator burden relative to membrane concentrate-producing systems
ozonation is well suited to improve removal of more oxidation-susceptible pesticides such as dichlorvos and can assist with tebuconazole control
BAC adds a robust polishing barrier for oxidation byproducts and residual organics while fitting reuse practice for advanced polishing
pretreatment addresses variable surface water turbidity and DOC, which is important for stable ozone performance and carbon bed life

although Metolachlor ethane sulfonic acid is relatively polar and may not be removed as strongly by adsorption alone, the combined ozone plus BAC barrier is a practical compromise under the stated constraints
potential_risks: Metolachlor ethane sulfonic acid removal may be incomplete if the utility relies on BAC adsorption rather than true separation, so pilot validation is needed
bromate formation potential must be assessed and controlled during ozonation because bromide is present
BAC performance depends on empty bed contact time, ozone dose, and management of biological activity
seasonal increases in DOC can raise ozone demand and shorten carbon replacement intervals

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove solids and reduce NOM loading
core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon: provide flexible seasonal adsorption boost for episodic pesticide peaks
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: polish dissolved organics and provide a relatively simple adsorptive barrier
core_unit_functions.UV_disinfection: final disinfection barrier for potable reuse

applicability_rationale: this route is operationally simpler than ozone or membrane-heavy systems and can often be managed by utilities familiar with conventional treatment
PAC can be seasonally adjusted to address spikes of dichlorvos and tebuconazole, while GAC provides continuing polishing
adsorption-based treatment avoids creation of a membrane concentrate stream and aligns with residuals preferences better than RO
the option is attractive where staff prefer familiar solids handling and media change-out over complex oxidation or membrane operations
potential_risks: Metolachlor ethane sulfonic acid is relatively polar and may exhibit weaker adsorption than parent hydrophobic pesticides, reducing confidence for very low reuse targets
higher DOC in surface water competes for adsorption sites and can materially increase PAC dose and GAC replacement frequency
PAC handling adds dust and slurry management requirements and increases solids residual production
performance can vary significantly with contact time and carbon selection

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: reduce fouling load before membrane treatment
core_unit_functions.nanofiltration: provide physical barrier for many dissolved organics including pesticides and some degradates
core_unit_functions.UV_advanced_oxidation: polish low-molecular-weight organics and add an advanced oxidation barrier
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust treated water chemistry for distribution or recharge compatibility

applicability_rationale: this route is technically stronger than adsorption-only options for variable multi-contaminant control and provides a true separation barrier
nanofiltration can be attractive when potable reuse reliability is weighted more heavily than simplicity
UV-AOP offers a polishing barrier for compounds not fully rejected or transformed upstream
the train is more likely than pure adsorption systems to control a polar degradate such as Metolachlor ethane sulfonic acid
potential_risks: membrane concentrate management is a major penalty under the stated residuals constraint
operator skill demands are higher for membrane integrity, cleaning optimization, and AOP process control
surface water fouling risk can increase downtime and chemical cleaning frequency
UV-AOP may be less cost-effective if oxidant demand from background water quality is significant

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove solids and reduce colloidal fouling potential
core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: provide high rejection of a broad suite of dissolved contaminants
core_unit_functions.UV_advanced_oxidation: polish permeate and add an additional advanced treatment barrier
core_unit_functions.stabilization: correct corrosivity and mineral balance after desalting

applicability_rationale: RO plus UV-AOP is a very strong potable reuse barrier and is technically capable of addressing mixed pesticide contamination with high confidence
this option is justified when regulatory conservatism and broad contaminant unknowns outweigh cost and concentrate concerns
it offers the most defensible barrier for a polar degradate that may be difficult for adsorption-based systems
potential_risks: the high-pressure membrane route conflicts with moderate budget and limited-operator constraints
concentrate disposal is a major implementation challenge and misaligns with the residuals preference
surface water applications require robust pretreatment and disciplined membrane maintenance to prevent fouling and scaling
post-treatment stabilization adds additional operational steps

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: reduce solids and NOM prior to advanced treatment
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: protect downstream UV reactors and carbon

applicability_rationale: this route is technically plausible as a non-membrane advanced treatment train and may be considered where ozone infrastructure is unavailable
UV-AOP can contribute to destruction of some pesticides and GAC provides a polishing step
potential_risks: UV-AOP is often less economical as a primary broad-spectrum trace-organic barrier for this water matrix than ozone-BAC
background DOC and scavenging can reduce oxidation efficiency and increase oxidant demand
GAC still may not reliably control Metolachlor ethane sulfonic acid to the same degree as membrane separation
process optimization is more demanding than simple adsorption but without the broad separation benefit of NF or RO

preferred_rank_pattern: a combined oxidation-plus-BAC route should rank near the top as the best compromise for mixed pesticide control and manageable complexity
adsorption-based conventional enhancement should appear as a strong but somewhat less reliable alternative because Metolachlor ethane sulfonic acid is more difficult to adsorb
NF_or_RO_based routes should appear as technically strong but downgraded by concentrate, cost, and operator-skill constraints
at least one technically capable membrane option should rank below simpler non-membrane options due to constraint misalignment

must_reflect: operator skill is limited to low-to-medium complexity systems rather than highly specialized membrane operations
moderate cost sensitivity prevents automatic selection of the highest-performance but highest-burden treatment train
residuals and concentrate handling materially influence ranking rather than contaminant removal alone
surface water variability requires pretreatment and robustness against DOC and turbidity swings

must_contain: why polar Metolachlor ethane sulfonic acid creates uncertainty for adsorption-only treatment
why ozone or UV-based oxidation can be attractive for dichlorvos and tebuconazole but still require polishing and byproduct control
why membrane options provide stronger contaminant barriers yet rank lower under concentrate and O&M constraints
why surface water DOC and turbidity affect carbon use, oxidation demand, and overall train selection

should_mention: competitive adsorption from natural organic matter
membrane fouling and concentrate disposal burden
seasonal variability in contaminant loading and source-water quality
uncertainty in degradate removal without pilot testing
